name: Axion-induced cosmic birefringence
summary: An axion-like field a coupled to electromagnetism through (a/f_a) F F-tilde (Chern-Simons term) causes left- and right-circular polarisations of light to propagate at slightly different speeds. CMB photons travelling from last scattering to today accumulate a polarisation rotation angle beta = (g_a gamma / 2) [a(today) - a(LSS)]. If beta = 0.3 degrees is real (Minami-Komatsu / Eskilt-Komatsu measurements), the axion field has slowly evolved by Delta a / f_a ~ 0.01 since recombination - consistent with ultralight dark energy or dark matter ALPs.

addresses_puzzles.note: Predicts beta in observed range for ULDM ALP (m_a ~ 10^-32 - 10^-28 eV) with f_a ~ M_Pl. Specific m_a determined by ALP-mass / Hubble-rate ratio: if m_a ~ H_0, the field is slowly rolling now; if m_a > H_0, oscillation washes out static beta but leaves an oscillation signature.
ULDM ALP can simultaneously constitute fuzzy DM and source cosmic birefringence; links two otherwise-independent anomalies.

If the ALP is quintessence-like (still rolling today), the birefringence is sensitive to its potential and field range. Quintessence + ALP-photon coupling links cosmic acceleration and CMB birefringence in one field.

touches_benchmarks.note: Predicts specific frequency-independent rotation that matches Minami-Komatsu / Eskilt-Komatsu measurement; future LiteBIRD + CMB-S4 sensitive to anisotropic-birefringence signatures.

introduces.note: ALP mass, photon-coupling strength, initial field value. Connects to existing string-axiverse model space.

history: Carroll, Field, Jackiw (1990), 'Limits on a Lorentz and parity violating modification of electrodynamics', Phys. Rev. D 41, 1231 - first identified the F F-dual rotation mechanism. Minami-Komatsu (2020) measurement renewed phenomenological interest. Modern ALP-birefringence model-building: Fujita-Murai-Nakatsuka-Tsujikawa (2020), arXiv:2008.02473; Choi et al. (2021).

references.citation: Carroll, Field, Jackiw, 'Limits on a Lorentz and parity violating modification of electrodynamics', Phys. Rev. D 41, 1231 (1990)
